Page | 4  

 
 
 1. Introduction 

This document provides basic information on using the Nimbus-4 Scanning Chopper Radiometer 
(SCR) Level-1 Radiance Data product. 

1.1 Data Product Description 

The Nimbus-4 Selective Chopper Radiometer (SCR) Level-1 Radiance Data product contains 
calibrated, earth-located radiances that were prepared by the experimenter's office on binary 
magnetic tapes. The radiances, measured by 6 channels at 2.3-15 micrometers with a ground 
resolution of 25 km, are "declouded" (interpolated and smoothed across regions of cloud). They are 
grouped into major frames along with orbit, altitude, latitude, longitude, and some ancillary data. 

Each file contains one day of data (~14 orbits per day). Spatial coverage is near global between 
latitude -80 and +80 degrees. The data are available from 1970/07/27 (day of year 208) through 
1973/01/30 (day of year 30). 

This product was previously available from the NASA National Space Science Data Center (NSSDC) 
under the name Selective Chopper Radiometer Radiance Files with the identifier ESAD-00238 (old id 
70-025A-10A). 

1.1.1 The Selective Chopper Radiometer 

Nimbus 4 Selective Chopper Radiometer observed the emitted infrared radiation in the 15-
micrometer absorption band of carbon dioxide. From these measurements the temperatures of six 
successive 10-km layers of the atmosphere were determined from earth or cloudtop level to 60-km 
height. : 

The SCR had six channels, which were arranged in three units of two. The four lower channels were 
called single-cell channels and used to define the depth of the atmosphere. The upper two channels, 
known as double-cell channels, were used to obtain adequate height resolution in the upper layers 
of the atmosphere. The output of each channel was sampled once every second. The upper two 
channels had a circular FOV approximately 160 km in diameter, and the lower four had a rectangular 
FOV about 112 km square. 

The channel 1 temperature monitoring system failed on June 15, 1970, thereby reducing the 
accuracy of the data. Channels 3 and 4 became noisy and unusable on April 18, 1972. The remaining 
channels were usable until June 15, 1973. 

Page | 5  

 
 
 The principal investigator for the SCR experiment was Prof. John T. Houghton from Oxford 
University. 

1.1.2 Nimbus-4 Overview 

The  Nimbus-4  satellite  was  successfully  launched  on  April  8,  1970.  The  spacecraft  included  nine 
experiments:  (1)  an  Image  Dissector  Camera  System  (IDCS)  for  providing  daytime  cloud  cover 
pictures,  both  in  real-time  and  recorded  modes  (2)  a  Temperature-Humidity  Infrared  Radiometer 
(THIR)  for  measuring  daytime  and  nighttime  surface  and  cloudtop  temperatures,  as  well  as  the 
water vapor content of the upper atmosphere, (3) an Backscatter Ultraviolet Spectrometer (BUV) for 
measuring  the  emission  spectra  of  the  earth/atmosphere  system,  (4)  a  Satellite  Infrared 
Spectrometer  (SIRS)  for  determining  the  vertical  profiles  of  temperature  and  water  vapor  in  the 
atmosphere, (5) a Monitor of Ultraviolet Solar Energy (MUSE) for detecting solar UV radiation, (6) a 
Backscatter Ultraviolet (BUV) detector for monitoring the vertical distribution and total amount of 
atmospheric  ozone  on  a  global  scale,  (7)  a  Filter  Wedge  Spectrometer  (FWS)  for  accurate 
measurement of IR radiance as a function of wavelength from the earth/atmosphere system, (8) a 
Selective  Chopper  Radiometer  (SCR)  for  determining  the  temperatures  of  six  successive  10-km 
layers in the atmosphere from absorption measurements in the 15-micrometer CO2 band, and (9) an 
Interrogation,  Recording,  and  Location  System  (IRLS)  for  locating,  interrogating,  recording,  and 
retransmitting meteorological and geophysical data from remote collection stations. 

The orbit of the satellite can be characterized by the following: 

  circular orbit at 1100 km 

  inclination of 80 degrees 

  period of an orbit is about 107 minutes 

  orbits cross the equator at 26 degrees of longitude separation 

  sun-synchronous 

1.2 Algorithm Background 

The  Nimbus-4  SCR  data  were  generated  from  the  spacecraft  telemetry,  attitude  and  orbital  data.  
The data were originally processed on IBM 360 computers using a 32-bit architecture, and copied to 
1600 bpi 9-track tapes for archival.  Further information on the SCR instrument and data processing 
can be found in the Nimbus-4 Users' Guide Section 9. 

Page | 6  

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 1.3 Data Disclaimer 

The data should be used with care and one should first read the Nimbus-4 User’s Guide, section 9 
describing the SCR experiment. Users should cite this data product in their research. 

Page | 7  

 
 
 2. Data Organization 

The Nimbus-4 Selective Chopper Radiometer Level-1 Radiance Data spans the time period from July 
27, 1970 to January 30, 1973. Each file contains one day (~14 orbits) of data. 

2.1 File Naming Convention 

The data product files are named according to the following convention: 

<Platform>-<Instrument>_<Product>_<DateTime>_<TapeNumber>.<Suffix> 

where: 
o Platform = name of the platform or satellite (always Nimbus4) 
o) Instrument = name of the instrument and product (always SCR) 
o) Product = process level (L1) 
o Date = Data start date and time in UTC in format <YYYY>m<MMDD>t<hhmmss> where 

1.  YYYY = 4 digit year (1970 – 1973) 
2.  MM = 2 digit month (01-12) 
3.  DD = 2 digit day of month (01-31) 
4.  hh = 2 digit hour (00-23) 
5.  mm = 2 digin minute (00-23) 
6.  ss = 2 digit second (00-59) 

o TapeNumber = 3 digit number of tape (preceeded by ‘DR’ - primary or ‘DS’ - backup) 
o Suffix = the file format (always TAP, indicating tape binary data) 

File name example: Nimbus4-SCR_L1_1970m0808t232921_DR227.TAP 

2.2 File Format and Structure 

The data are stored as they were originally written in IBM binary (big-endian) record oriented 
structured files. The files were written on the original 1600 bpi 9-track tapes using a blocked 
FORTRAN format. Note, the first record contains just the first FORTRAN record size header word and 
contains the Orbit Header. Subsequent records contain both the FORTAN record leading and trailing 
size header words. A file typically contains a full day of data or 14 orbits. 

Each day file contains a day header record/file, followed by up to 15 orbit files, and ending with an 
end of day record/file. An orbit file starts with an Orbit Header Record (size 28 bytes), followed by a 
Calibration and Housekeeping Record (size 526 bytes), a set of Data Records (each of size 3714 
bytes), and ending with a End of Orbit Record (size 18 or 14 bytes). Each Data Record represents a 
major frame and consists of up to 16 logical records or profile measurements. For the contents and 
layout of the documentation, see section 3.1 

Page | 8  

 
 
 
 
 
 There are 717 unique daily data files in this collection. All but one of the files come from the primary 
tape copies, the exception is day 1970/07/27 which is from the backup tape and contains one extra 
data record not found on the primary tape file. 

2.3 Key Science Data Fields 

The primary science data fields in this data product are the SCR calibrated radiances in units of 
mW/m2/sr/cm-1 for each channel. 

Figure 1: Typical data coverage of a Nimbus 4 SCR Level 1 daily data file. 

Page | 9  

 
 
 
 
 3. Data Contents 

The granularity of this data product is one day (with approx. 14 orbits). 

3.1 Data Records 

The Nimbus-4 User’s Guide does not describe the layout of the file format. Refer instead to the data 
provider’s documentation “Clarendon Laboratory Atmospheric Physics, The Selective Chopper 
Radiometer on Nimbus IV Archived Data”. 

The original tape files each included a tape summary file consisting of a summary header, and 
summary day records. These were then followed by a set of up to thirty day header record files, 
which would include a set of up to 16 orbit files and an end of day record file. As part of the 
recovery, the GES DISC has extracted and archived the day and orbit files as a single daily file. The 
tape summary files are not included. The original data were written using 6-bit bytes. During tape 
data recovery these were saved as 8-bit bytes (bits 6 and 7 are thus unused), a word is two 6-bit 
bytes. Characters are in the IBM 48 character BCD encoding. The tables below summarize the file 
record types. Each record type below starts and ends with a four byte word giving the record size in 
bytes.

 3.2 Metadata 

The metadata are contained in a separate XML formatted file having the same name as the data file 
with .xml appended to it. 

Table 3-2: Metadata attributes associated with the data file. 
Name

YYYY-MM-DD and time is hh-mm-ss.

Page | 14  

 
 4. Reading the Data 

The data are written in a binary record-oriented format. Using the record format specification in the 
section above, users can write software to read the data files. Please note that the data were 
originally written using a big-endian format, therefore users on little-endian machines will need to 
swap bytes for the words. 

A sample FORTRAN program is included in the Appendix section which will read in the data records. 
Additionally a FORTRAN function is included to perform byte swapping. 

Page | 15  

 
 
 5. Data Services 

5.1 GES DISC Search 

The GES DISC provides a keyword, spatial, temporal and advanced (event) searches through its 
unified search and download interface: 

https://disc.gsfc.nasa.gov/ 

5.2 Documentation 

The data product landing pages provide information about these data products, as well as links to 
download the data files and relevant documentation: 

https://disc.gsfc.nasa.gov/datacollection/SCRN4L1_001.html 

5.3 Direct Download 

These data products are available for users to download directly using HTTPS: 

https://acdisc.gesdisc.eosdis.nasa.gov/data/Nimbus4_SCR_Level1/SCRN4L1.001/ 

Page | 16

 FORTRAN Code 

C----------------------------------------------------------------------- 

C----------------------------------------------------------------------- 
C ^NAME: READ_SCR 
C   This program will read an entire Nimbus 4 SCR Level-1 data file. 
C 
C   The SCR data contain an initial orbit header record, followed by a 
C   housekeeping record, series of data records. Each of 
C   data record contains a data header and a set of six scan line records. 
C   The function can optionally display the temperature data on a cylindrical 
C   map, and this can then be sent to an output PNG file. The geolocation 
C   metadata may also be overlayed on the map. 
C 
C ^MAJOR VARIABLES: 
C     FNAME  - name of input file 
C     BLOCK  - buffer for data block typically has three data records 
C     BUFF   - buffer for holding temporary 4-byte word 
C     WORD   - integer 4-byte word 
C     IBLKSZ - size of block in bytes 
C     IOS    - I/O status number 
C 
C ^NOTES: 
C     Compile: gfortran -o READ_SCR.EXE READ_SCR.FOR 
C 
C ^ORGANIZATION: NASA/GSFC, Code 610.2 
C 
C ^AUTHOR:  James Johnson 
C 
C ^ADDRESS: james.johnson@nasa.gov 
C 
C ^CREATED: August 29, 2017 
C----------------------------------------------------------------------- 

      CHARACTER          FNAME*1024 
      CHARACTER          BLOCK(10000)  ! Buffer = 8064 bytes 
      INTEGER*2          IWORD(5000) 
      CHARACTER          BUFF(4)       ! Buffer to hold 4-byte word 
      INTEGER*4          WORD          ! 4-byte word 
      INTEGER*4          IBLKSZ        ! Block size header 
      INTEGER*4          IRECSZ        ! Size of logical records 
      INTEGER*4          NRECS         ! Number of logical records 
      EQUIVALENCE        (BUFF, WORD) 

C     Get the name of the input data file to read 
      WRITE (0, *), 'Enter the name of the input file:' 
      READ (5,'(A)') FNAME 
      PRINT '("File = ",A)', FNAME 

C     Open the specified input file 
      OPEN (UNIT=1, FILE=FNAME, STATUS='OLD', ACCESS='DIRECT', 
     &      FORM='UNFORMATTED', RECL=1, ERR=99, IOSTAT=IOS) 

C     Initialize N (block number) and IOFF (byte offset in file) 
      N=1 
Page | 19  

 
 
 
 
 
 
       IOFF=0 

C     Loop through the file reading all blocks of data 
    5 DO 
C       Read the first 4-byte word or block size header 
        DO I=1,4 
          READ (1, REC=IOFF+I, IOSTAT=IOS, ERR=90) BUFF(I) 
        END DO 
        IBLKSZ = WORD 
        IOFF=IOFF+(I-1) 

        IF (IBLKSZ .EQ. 0) THEN 
C         PRINT '("WARNING: END-OF-TAPE MARK")' 
          GOTO 5 
        ENDIF 

C       Next read the block of data 
        DO I=1,IBLKSZ 
          READ (1, REC=IOFF+I, IOSTAT=IOS) BLOCK(I) 
          IF (IOS .NE. 0) THEN 
            PRINT '("ERROR: BLOCK ",I4,X,I4,", IOSTAT: ",I6)', N,I-1,IOS 
            IBLKSZ = I-1 
            GOTO 20 
          ENDIF 

C         Data were written as 12-bit words, store in 2-byte integers 
          IF (MOD(I+1,2) .EQ. 0) THEN 
            IWORD((I+1)/2) = 0          ! Initialize IWORD to zero 
            IWORD((I+1)/2) = IWORD((I+1)/2) + ISHFT(ICHAR(BLOCK(I)),6) 
          ELSE 
            IWORD((I+1)/2) = IWORD((I+1)/2) + ICHAR(BLOCK(I)) 
          ENDIF 

        END DO 
        IOFF=IOFF+(I-1) 
        N=N+1 

C       The record identifier is word 5 
        IF      (IWORD(5) .EQ. '4200'O) THEN 
          CALL PR4200(IWORD, IBLKSZ, N) 
        ELSE IF (IWORD(5) .EQ. '4201'O) THEN 
          CALL PR4201(IWORD, IBLKSZ, N) 
        ELSE IF (IWORD(5) .EQ. '4202'O) THEN 
          CALL PR4202(IWORD, IBLKSZ, N) 
        ELSE IF (IWORD(5) .EQ. '4203'O) THEN 
          CALL PR4203(IWORD, IBLKSZ, N) 
        ELSE IF (IWORD(5) .EQ. '4204'O) THEN 
          CALL PR4204(IWORD, IBLKSZ, N) 
        ELSE IF (IWORD(5) .EQ. '4205'O) THEN 
          CALL PR4205(IWORD, IBLKSZ, N) 
        ELSE IF (IWORD(5) .EQ. '4206'O) THEN 
          CALL PR4206(IWORD, IBLKSZ, N) 
        ELSE IF (IWORD(5) .EQ. '4207'O) THEN 
          CALL PR4206(IWORD, IBLKSZ, N) 
        ELSE 
          PRINT '("Unknown record identifier: ", O4.4)', IWORD(5) 
        ENDIF 

Page | 20  

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 C       Finally read the last 4-byte word 
C       (should match first block size) 
   20   DO I=1,4 
          READ (1, REC=IOFF+I, IOSTAT=IOS, ERR=90) BUFF(I) 
        END DO 
        IF (IBLKSZ .NE. WORD) THEN 
          PRINT '("WARNING: IBLKSZ ",I10," != ",I10)', WORD, IBLKSZ 
        ENDIF 
        IOFF=IOFF+(I-1) 

      END DO 

C     Close the input file 
   90 CLOSE(1) 
      GOTO 100 

   99 PRINT '("ERROR: OPEN FILE, IOSTAT: ",I6)', IOS 

  100 STOP 
      END 

C----------------------------------------------------------------------- 
C     This Subroutine will print Summary Head Record (4200) 
C----------------------------------------------------------------------- 
      SUBROUTINE PR4200(WRDARR, IBLKSZ, N) 

      INTEGER*2          WRDARR(500)   ! Word Array 
      CHARACTER          BUFF*4        ! Temporary data buffer 

      PRINT '("******************")' 

C     First two words are some kind of file marker 
      PRINT '("SYNC   =",2(X,O4.4))', WRDARR(1:2) 
C     Record length 
      PRINT '("RECLEN =",X,I6)', WRDARR(3) 
C     Record number 
      PRINT '("RECNUM =",X,I6)', WRDARR(4) 
C     Record identifier 
      PRINT '("RECID  =",X,O4.4)', WRDARR(5) 
C     Day number 
      PRINT '("NDAYS  =",X,I6)', WRDARR(6) 
C     EOR 
      PRINT '("EOR    =",X,O4.4)', WRDARR(7) 
C     Checksum 
      PRINT '("CHKSUM =",X,I6)', WRDARR(8) 

      RETURN 
      END 

C----------------------------------------------------------------------- 
C     This Subroutine will print Summary Day Record (4201) 
C----------------------------------------------------------------------- 
      SUBROUTINE PR4201(WRDARR, IBLKSZ, N) 

Page | 21  

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
       INTEGER*2          WRDARR(500)   ! Word Array 
      CHARACTER          BUFF*4        ! Temporary data buffer 
      INTEGER*4          I4BUFF        ! 4-byte integer buffer 
      CHARACTER          HDRSS(2) 

      PRINT '("******************")' 

C     First two words are some kind of file marker 
      PRINT '("SYNC   =",2(X,O4.4))', WRDARR(1:2) 
C     Record length 
      PRINT '("RECLEN =",X,I6)', WRDARR(3) 
C     Record number 
      PRINT '("RECNUM =",X,I6)', WRDARR(4) 
C     Record identifier 
      PRINT '("RECID  =",X,O4.4)', WRDARR(5) 
C     Day number 
      PRINT '("DAY    =",X,I6)', WRDARR(6) 
C     Year 
      PRINT '("YEAR   =",X,I6)', WRDARR(7) 
C     Number of orbits (max 16) 
      PRINT '("NORBIT =",X,I6)', WRDARR(8) 
      DO I=1,WRDARR(8) 
        IOFF=(I-1)*11 
        PRINT '("------------------")' 
C       Orbit number 
        I4BUFF = ISHFT(WRDARR(IOFF+9),12) + WRDARR(IOFF+10) 
        IF (I4BUFF .LT. 0) THEN 
          I4BUFF = 65536 + I4BUFF 
        ENDIF 
        PRINT '("ORBNUM =",X,I8)', I4BUFF 
C       HDRSS  
        J = ISHFT(WRDARR(IOFF+11),-6) 
        HDRSS(1) = CHAR(IBCD(J)) 
        K = IAND(WRDARR(IOFF+11),'003F'Z) 
        HDRSS(2) = CHAR(IBCD(K)) 
        PRINT '("HDRSS  =",2(X,A1))', HDRSS 
C       Day Number 
        PRINT '("DAYNUM =",X,I6)', WRDARR(IOFF+12) 
C       Time (GMT) 
        I4BUFF = ISHFT(WRDARR(IOFF+13),12) + WRDARR(IOFF+14) 
        IF (I4BUFF .LT. 0) THEN 
          I4BUFF = 65536 + I4BUFF 
        ENDIF 
        PRINT '("TIME   =",X,I8)', I4BUFF 
C       Number of Major Frames 
        PRINT '("NMAJFR =",X,I6)', WRDARR(IOFF+15) 
C       Number of bad checksums 
        PRINT '("NBADCK =",X,I6)', WRDARR(IOFF+16) 
C       Number of 1st Good Major Frame 
        PRINT '("N1STMF =",X,I6)', WRDARR(IOFF+17) 
C       Number of Good THIR Records 
        PRINT '("NGTHIR =",X,I6)', WRDARR(IOFF+18) 
C       Flagword 
        PRINT '("FLAGWD =",X,B6.6)', WRDARR(IOFF+19) 
      END DO 
      PRINT '("------------------")' 
C     EOR 

Page | 22  

 
 
 
       PRINT '("EOR    =",X,O4.4)', WRDARR(IBLKSZ/2-1) 
C     Checksum 
      PRINT '("CHKSUM =",X,I6)', WRDARR(IBLKSZ/2) 

      RETURN 
      END

C     Spare

C----------------------------------------------------------------------- 
C     This Subroutine will print the Data Record (4205) 
C----------------------------------------------------------------------- 
      SUBROUTINE PR4205(WRDARR, IBLKSZ, N) 

      INTEGER*2          WRDARR(500)   ! Word Array 
      CHARACTER          BUFF*4        ! Temporary data buffer 
      INTEGER*4          I4BUFF        ! 4-byte integer buffer 
      CHARACTER          HDRSS(2) 

      PRINT '("******************")' 

C     First two words are some kind of file marker 
      PRINT '("SYNC   =",2(X,O4.4))', WRDARR(1:2) 
C     Record length 
      PRINT '("RECLEN =",X,I6)', WRDARR(3) 
C     Record number 
      PRINT '("RECNUM =",X,I6)', WRDARR(4) 
C     Record identifier 
      PRINT '("RECID  =",X,O4.4)', WRDARR(5) 
C     Number of internal records (max 16) 
      PRINT '("NUMINT =",X,I6)', WRDARR(6) 
C     Internal record length 
      PRINT '("INTLEN =",X,I6)', WRDARR(7) 
      DO I=1,WRDARR(6) 
        IOFF=(I-1)*WRDARR(7) 
        PRINT '("------------------")' 
C       Orbit number 
        I4BUFF = ISHFT(WRDARR(IOFF+8),12) + WRDARR(IOFF+9) 
        IF (I4BUFF .LT. 0) THEN 
          I4BUFF = 65536 + I4BUFF 
        ENDIF 
        PRINT '("ORBNUM =",X,I8)', I4BUFF 
C       HDRSS  
        J = ISHFT(WRDARR(IOFF+10),-6) 
        HDRSS(1) = CHAR(IBCD(J)) 
        K = IAND(WRDARR(IOFF+10),'003F'Z) 
        HDRSS(2) = CHAR(IBCD(K)) 
        PRINT '("HDRSS  =",2(X,A1))', HDRSS 

Page | 25  

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 C       Transmitted Record Number 
        PRINT '("XMTNUM =",X,I6)', WRDARR(IOFF+11) 
C       Major Frame Number 
        PRINT '("FRMNUM =",X,I6)', WRDARR(IOFF+12) 
C       Day number 
        PRINT '("DAYNUM =",X,I6)', WRDARR(IOFF+13) 
C       Time (GMT) 
        I4BUFF = ISHFT(WRDARR(IOFF+14),12) + WRDARR(IOFF+15) 
        IF (I4BUFF .LT. 0) THEN 
          I4BUFF = 65536 + I4BUFF 
        ENDIF 
        PRINT '("TIME   =",X,I8)', I4BUFF 
C       Latitude  (if over 90 subtract 2**12 to make negative, scaled by 8) 
        I4BUFF = WRDARR(IOFF+16) 
        IF (I4BUFF .GT. 90*8) THEN 
          I4BUFF = I4BUFF - 2**12 
        ENDIF 
        PRINT '("LAT    =",X,F8.3)', REAL(I4BUFF)/8 
C       Longitude (scaled by 8) 
        PRINT '("LON    =",X,F8.3)', REAL(WRDARR(IOFF+17))/8 
C       THIR (raw-packed) 
        PRINT '("THRAW  =",/,10(X,I6))', WRDARR(IOFF+18:IOFF+71) 
C       Housekeeping Averages 
        PRINT '("HKAVG  =",2(X,I6))', WRDARR(IOFF+72:IOFF+73) 
C       THIR bolometer (unpacked) 
        PRINT '("THBOLO =",2(X,I6))', WRDARR(IOFF+74:IOFF+75) 
C       Altitude (scaled by 8 offset by 1000) 
        PRINT '("ALT    =",X,F8.3)', REAL(WRDARR(IOFF+76))/8+1000 
C       THIR max/min/mean 
        PRINT '("THSTAT =",3(X,I6))', WRDARR(IOFF+77:IOFF+79) 
C       Flags 
        PRINT '("FLAGS  =",2(X,B12.12))', WRDARR(IOFF+80:IOFF+81) 
C       Radiances (scaled by 16) 
        PRINT '("RAD    =",7(X,F9.4))', REAL(WRDARR(IOFF+82:IOFF+88))/16 
C       Channel A,B radiances imbalance corrected 
        PRINT '("RADIMB =",2(X,F9.4))', REAL(WRDARR(IOFF+89:IOFF+90))/16 
C       Analogue housekeeping 
        PRINT '("ANLGHK =",/,10(X,I6))', WRDARR(IOFF+91:IOFF+139) 
      END DO 
      PRINT '("------------------")' 
C     EOR 
      PRINT '("EOR    =",X,O4.4)', WRDARR(IBLKSZ/2-1) 
C     Checksum 
      PRINT '("CHKSUM =",X,I6)', WRDARR(IBLKSZ/2) 

      RETURN 
      END

C----------------------------------------------------------------------- 
C     This Function will return BCD to ASCII character index 
C----------------------------------------------------------------------- 
      FUNCTION IBCD(I) 

      CHARACTER BCDTBL(64) 
C                  _0  _1  _2  _3  _4  _5  _6  _7   
      DATA BCDTBL /' ','1','2','3','4','5','6','7', ! 0_ 
     +             '8','9','0','#','@','|','|','|', ! 1_ 
     +             '|','/','S','T','U','V','W','X', ! 2_ 
     +             'Y','Z','|',',','%','|','|','|', ! 3_ 
     +             '-','J','K','L','M','N','O','P', ! 4_ 
     +             'Q','R','|','$','*','|','|','|', ! 5_ 
     +             '&','A','B','C','D','E','F','G', ! 6_ 
     +             'H','I','|','.','_','|','|','|'/ ! 7_ 

      IF (BCDTBL(I+1) .EQ. '|') THEN 
        IBCD = 0   ! Null 
      ELSE IF (BCDTBL(I+1) .EQ. '_') THEN 
        IBCD = 255 ! Square Lozenge Unavailabe in ASCII 
      ELSE 
        IBCD = ICHAR(BCDTBL(I+1)) 
      ENDIF 

      RETURN 
      END
